coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots combined with masking agent for high selectivity detection of Cr(VI) to overcome interference associated challenges
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Shengliang
Lai, Xuandi
Liu, Feiyan
Chen, Liqiong
coredata.dc:description: Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) determination is of great importance to the public health because of its extensive sources and high toxicity. However, interference from non-target ions and complex matrix remains challenges for Cr(VI) detection. In this work, we constructed a novel sensing system for high selectivity detection of Cr(VI), which is composed of strong emitting carbon dots (CE-CDs) and a specific masking agent. The detection conditions, anti-interference capability and the sensing and masking mechanisms of CE-CDs-based sensing method were systematically investigated. The results revealed that the optimal detection conditions included pH 4–10, reaction time 180 s and CE-CDs concentration 18 mg/L. Under optimal conditions, the linear range of the method was up to 500 µm, and the detection limit was as low as 23 nM. In addition, the interference of Hg(II) can be accurately eliminated by using DMPS, an effective masking agent. During the sensing process, inner filter effect and ion-molecular interaction between Cr(VI) and CE-CDs accounted for the fluorescence quenching mechanism, while the efficient masking was attributed to the strong coordination interaction between Hg(II) and DMPS. Most notably, this method had broad applicability, even for the trace detection of Cr(VI) in colored leather with complex matrix. These findings indicate that this approach is expected to open up new avenues for Cr(VI) detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
High quantum yield
Hexavalent chromium
Fluorometric analysis
Anti-interference

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) One-step hydrothermal synthesis of the CE-CDs. (b) TEM image and (c) XPS spectrum of the CE-CDs. The inset of (b) shows the high-resolution TEM image of the CE-CDs. (d) C 1 s and (e) N 1 s spectra of the CE-CDs.

(a) UV–vis spectrum, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of the CE-CDs. The insets are digital images of the CE-CD solution under (left) white light and (right) 365 nm UV light. (b) Fluorescence intensity of the CE-CDs within 30 days storage at under 4 ℃. (c) Original and (d) normalized fluorescence emission spectra of the CE-CDs under different excitation wavelengths.

Fluorescence emission spectra of the CE-CDs incubated with different ions (a) in the absence or (b) in the presence of DMPS. Insets are digital images of the samples under 365 nm UV light. (c) Fluorescence intensity of the CE-CDs, CE-CDs+Hg(II), and CE-CDs+Cr(VI) under different concentrations of DMPS. (d) Fluorescence intensity of the CE-CDs incubated with interference ions or incubated with interference ions and Cr(VI) in the presence of 0.1 M DMPS.

The FQE (calculated as (F
0
-F)/F
0 ) of Cr(VI) to the CE-CDs under (a) different CE-CD concentrations and (b) different incubation times. Fluorescence emission spectra of (c) 900 mg/L or (e) 18 mg/L CE-CDs incubated with different concentrations of Cr(VI). Inset of (c) shows the digital image of the samples under 365 nm UV light. (d) and (f) are the fluorescence intensities at 442 nm recorded from (c) and (e), respectively. The insets show the linear fitting diagrams.

(a) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the CE-CDs and UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI). (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of CE-CDs, Cr(VI), and CE-CDs+Cr(VI). (c) Fluorescence decay curves of the CE-CDs and CE-CDs+Cr(VI). (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CE-CDs, Hg(II), CE-CDs+Hg(II), and CE-CDs+DMPS+Hg(II). (e) XPS spectra of DMPS and DMPS+Hg(II) and (f) the content of each peak belonging to S p3/2, S p1/2, and Sx.
Schematic diagram of the CE-CDs-based fluorescence sensor for the selective detection of Cr(VI).
Recoveries of Cr(VI) from leather samples and real-world water samples (Spiked) using the CE-CDs-based sensing system and ion chromatography (IC method), respectively. (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots combined with masking agent for high selectivity detection of Cr(VI) to overcome interference associated challenges

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of New Materials and New Energies, Shenzhen Technology University, Shenzhen 518118, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of New Materials and New Energies, Shenzhen Technology University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Applied Sciences, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Applied Sciences, Shenzhen University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Applied Sciences, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analysis and Testing Center, Shenzhen Technology University, Shenzhen 518118, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Analysis and Testing Center, Shenzhen Technology University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analysis and Testing Center, Shenzhen Technology University, Shenzhen 518118, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of New Materials and New Energies, Shenzhen Technology University, Shenzhen 518118, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of New Materials and New Energies, Shenzhen Technology University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) determination is of great importance to the public health because of its extensive sources and high toxicity. However, interference from non-target ions and complex matrix remains challenges for Cr(VI) detection. In this work, we constructed a novel sensing system for high selectivity detection of Cr(VI), which is composed of strong emitting carbon dots (CE-CDs) and a specific masking agent. The detection conditions, anti-interference capability and the sensing and masking mechanisms of CE-CDs-based sensing method were systematically investigated. The results revealed that the optimal detection conditions included pH 4–10, reaction time 180 s and CE-CDs concentration 18 mg/L. Under optimal conditions, the linear range of the method was up to 500 µm, and the detection limit was as low as 23 nM. In addition, the interference of Hg(II) can be accurately eliminated by using DMPS, an effective masking agent. During the sensing process, inner filter effect and ion-molecular interaction between Cr(VI) and CE-CDs accounted for the fluorescence quenching mechanism, while the efficient masking was attributed to the strong coordination interaction between Hg(II) and DMPS. Most notably, this method had broad applicability, even for the trace detection of Cr(VI) in colored leather with complex matrix. These findings indicate that this approach is expected to open up new avenues for Cr(VI) detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution mainly occurs due to industrial wastewater and has become a major environmental problem that affects agricultural safety and human health (Vardhan et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2022a,b, 2019). Among numerous heavy metal ions, including hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)), have attracted much attention due to their large-scale use, high level of toxicity, and non-degradation (Duan et al., 2020; Kishore et al., 2017). Chromium is widely used in various industries, such as electroplating, leather making, dyestuff, and steel making (Gong et al., 2017). As a result, excess Cr(VI) is discharged into environment, and can accumulate in the ecosystem and human body by moving through the water cycle and food chains (Tian et al., 2017). Moreover, the Cr(VI) and its compounds invade the human body through ingestion, inhalation, and contact with skin, causing immune system disorders, metabolic impairment, gene alteration, and even carcinogenicity (Zhang et al., 2022). Therefore, the monitoring of the content of Cr(VI) ions in the environment is important for public health.

Traditionally, instrumental analysis methods, including chromatography (Threeprom et al., 2007), atomic absorption spectrometry (Zeng et al., 2012), and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) (Chen et al., 2020), have been used for chromium ion detection. However, these methods are unable to distinguish between trivalent chromium (Cr(III)) and Cr(VI). In addition, Cr(VI) is easily reduced to Cr(III), which is less toxic (Vellaichamy et al., 2017). These methods also have the disadvantages of being expensive instruments, complex operation processes, time consuming and high cost, and are not conducive to rapid on-site testing of Cr(VI). Therefore, there is an urgent need for the development of fast, convenient, and low-cost on-site detection methods for Cr(VI) detection.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn much attention for their use as fluorescence sensors due to attractive features, such as simple and economical synthesis, good water solubility, and excellent fluorescence properties (Wu and Tong, 2020; Zeng et al., 2022). In terms of Cr(VI) detection, various sensors based on the fluorescence quenching effect of Cr(VI) on the CDs have been developed (Li et al., 2018a). Meanwhile, some effort has been made to improve the detection efficiency of CD-based fluorescence sensors, including increasing the quantum yield (QY) of the CDs through nitrogen doping (Zheng et al., 2021). Since nitrogen atoms have a comparable atomic size and five valence electrons available for combining with carbon atoms, nitrogen doping can introduce defect sites and provide potential active sites, which could effectively tune the intrinsic photoluminescence performance of the CDs (Li and Dong, 2018). Nevertheless, the applicability of the CD-based fluorescence sensors for Cr(VI) detection remains to be further investigated for samples with a complex matrix or containing a variety of heavy metal ions. Most typically, the selectivity experiments involved in most studies conducted have barely investigated the interference effect of Hg(II) on the detection of Cr(VI), although these two ions are of similar origin (Pratush et al., 2018). In fact, the currently developed CD-based Cr(VI) sensors either fail to consider the interference of the complex matrix in real samples and the presence of Hg(II), or fail to clarify the mechanism of sensing selectivity. Therefore, the above challenges require an unambiguous understanding of the sensitivity and selectivity mechanism of the CD-based detection of Cr(VI) in a complicated environment.

In this study, a fluorescence sensor consisting of nitrogen-doped CDs (CE-CDs) and Hg(II) specific masking agent DMPS were constructed for Cr(VI) detection. The sensing mechanism of this fluorescence sensor is illustrated in Scheme 1. Because of the sensitive response of the CE-CDs to Cr(VI) and the accurate masking of Hg(II) by the DMPS, the as-constructed fluorescence sensor exhibited superior selectivity and sensitivity to Cr(VI) with a wide detection range (0–500 μM) and low detection limit (23 nM). The sensing and masking mechanisms of the sensor were further investigated, which revealed that the inner filter effect and ion-molecular interactions accounted for the fluorescence quenching mechanism, while efficient masking was attributed to the strong coordination interaction between Hg(II) and DMPS. It's worth mentioning that the fluorescence sensor can be applied for the determination of Cr(VI) in complex real samples, including tap water, river water, and in particular colored leather samples, with a high recovery rate. This study provides a clear perspective on the development of CD-based fluorescence sensors with high efficiency and wide applicability.

HCl, NaOH, citric acid monohydrate, sodium 2,3-dimercaptopropane-1-sulfonate (DMPS), L-cysteine (LML), Phosphate buffer solution (PBS), K2Cr2O7 and all the interference ions were all purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine monohydrate was obtained from Acmec Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium thiocyanide (NaSCN, SDT) was bought from Mread Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All reagents are of analytical grade and used directly without further purification. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (25 °C, 18.2 MΩ).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) comes from FEI Tecnai G2 Spirit electron microscope (accelerating voltage, 120 kV). High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were obtained by a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 300 kV. The Fourier Infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured by Shimadzu IRAffinity-1S spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed by PHI 5000 Versaprobe-II Focus X-ray Photo-electron Spectroscopy. The UV–vis spectrum is measured by Shimadzu UV-2700 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectrum was measured by Shimadzu RF-6000 spectro fluorophotometer. The fluorescence decay curve is measured by Edinburgh FLS1000 Spectrometer. Ion chromatography data is measured by Thermo Fisher Scientific Dionex Integrion RFIC.

The CE-CDs were prepared via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method, as described in previous reports with some modifications (Xu et al., 2016). Typically, 0.9 g of citric acid monohydrate was dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water and 1.378 mL of ethylenediamine monohydrate was added under stirring. Then, the mixed solution was transferred into 50 mL of Teflon-lined autoclave and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 5 h. The resultant solution was subjected to dialysis (MWCO: 500 Da) in ultrapure water for 12 h in the dark. Finally, the purified product was dried under vacuum to obtain the CE-CD powder.

The QY of the CE-CDs was measured using quinine sulfate as a standard fluorescent substance. In brief, the absorbance of quinine sulfate (1, 2, 4, and 5 mg/L) or the CE-CDs (22.5, 45.0, 90.0 and 135.0 mg/L) at 350 nm was determined using an UV–vis spectrometer. Then, the fluorescence spectra of various concentrations of CE-CDs and quinine sulfate were measured under an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, and their integrated fluorescence intensities were calculated separately. The QY of the CE-CDs was calculated using the following formula (Zheng et al., 2021):
QY =
QY
ref
×
η
2
I
A
ref
η
ref
2
I
ref
A
where QY
ref is the quantum yield of quinine sulfate (QY=55%), η refers to the refractive index of the solvent, I is the integrated fluorescence intensity (FI), A is the UV–vis absorbance at 350 nm. The subscript ref refers to parameters of quinine sulfate.

To investigate the response specificity of the CE-CDs to Cr(VI), the fluorescence spectra of the CE-CDs conjugated with different ions, including Cr(VI), were measured. Typically, 490 μL of the 500 μM ion solution was mixed with 10 μL of 900 mg/L CE-CDs. After 5 min of incubation at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra under a 350 nm excitation wavelength were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. To eliminate the influence of Hg(II) ions, masking agents, including NaSCN (Mehta and Kailasa, 2015), LML (Zhu and Zhang, 2014), and DMPS (Campbell et al., 1986), were used and the fluorescence images of sample under the absence or presence of masking agents under a 365 nm UV light were obtained. The results showed that DMPS exerted good masking capability on Hg(II), and was chosen as the masking agent to be used in the subsequent studies.

Unless otherwise stated, all analyses were performed in water containing 0.2 mM DMPS at room temperature. For the quantitative analysis, 3 μL of DMPS (0.1 M) and 10 μL of the CE-CDs (18 mg/L) were mixed in a centrifuge tube, and then 487 μL of Cr(VI) solution (0.1–1000 μM) was added. After 180 s of incubation, the fluorescence emission spectrum of each sample was measured at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. All experiments were performed in triplicate. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (FQE) was determined using the following formula (Song et al., 2018):
FQE =
F
0
− F
F
0
Where F
0 and F refer to the FI of CE-CDs in the absence and presence of Cr(VI), respectively.

Accordingly, the selectivity experiments were conducted by adding various interference ions into the CE-CDs with or without Cr(VI). The interference ions used here included Cu(II), Ba(II), Mn(II), Ni(II), Mg(II), K(I), Cr(III), Hg(II), Ca(II), Pb(II), Na(I), Cd(II), Al(III), SO4 2-, and PO4 3-.

The water samples were collected from a tap at our laboratory and the Pingshan River in Shenzhen, China. All water samples were boiled for 5 min to remove excess chlorine and microorganisms, and then centrifugated at 13,000 rpm for 30 min to remove bulk deposits. Finally, the samples were filtered through a 0.45 µm membrane. Related parameters of the pretreated water sample were as follows: the pH was 8.18, the total dissolved solids (TDS) value was 306 mg/L, the total suspended solids (TSS) was less than 4 mg/L, and the hardness was 1.29 mmol/L. The leather samples were pretreated using the following steps (Luo et al., 2019): 2.00 g of leather was cut into small pieces and added to 10 mL of the phosphate buffer (pH=8.0). After shaking for 3 h in a nitrogen atmosphere, the solution was filtered and the pH was adjusted to 7.0–8.0. The recovery efficiency of the CE-CDs-based sensing method was evaluated by conducting spiked recovery experiments. For validation, the collected real samples were analyzed using the standard ion chromatograph method.

The nitrogen doped CE-CDs derived from citric acid and ethylenediamine were prepared using a one-pot hydrothermal method, in which the ethylenediamine provided the nitrogen source ( Fig. 1a). The as-prepared CE-CDs were well-dispersed in the water with a nearly spherical morphology as observed under TEM (Fig. 1b) and the average diameter of the CE-CDs was measured to be 4.24 nm (Fig. S1). High-resolution TEM images show that the CE-CDs had well-arranged lattice fringes with 0.23 nm lattice spacing (inset of Fig. 1b), which corresponded to the (100) facet of sp2 graphite carbon (Liu et al., 2015). Moreover, the XRD spectrum of the CE-CDs exhibited a broad peak around 22.5° (Fig. S2), which can be assigned to the (002) lattice spacing of graphite carbon, indicating that the CE-CDs are carbon-based (Jin et al., 2015). The surface functional groups of the CE-CDs were analyzed using XPS. Fig. 1c shows the full XPS spectrum of the CE-CDs and the typical intense peaks observed at 285.55, 398.96, and 531.98 eV correspond to C (64.2 %), N (18.0 %), and O (17.8 %), respectively. The ratio of N/C in the CE-CDs was as high as 28 %, which can be beneficial for its quantum yield (Bardhan et al., 2020). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum showed that the peaks were centered at 284.32, 285.20, 286.13, and 287.52 eV, indicating the presence of C＝C/C—C, C—N, C—O, and C＝O (Fig. 1d) (Jiang et al., 2015). Additionally, the peaks centered around 398.60, 399.37, and 400.73 eV in the high-resolution N 1s spectrum can be attributed to C—N, pyridinic N, and graphitic N (Fig. 1e) (Wang et al., 2018).

In addition, the FTIR spectrometer was used to further explore the structural information of the CE-CD (Fig. S3). The broad absorption bands at 3361 and 3264 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of O—H and N—H, and the bands at 2931 and 2885 cm−1 can arebe the characteristic peaks of C—H (Wang et al., 2020). Sharp peaks at 1653, 1560, and 1390 cm−1 are ascribed to the bending vibrations of C＝O, N—H, and C—N bonds, respectively (Zhao et al., 2016). Based on the above results, the high nitrogen doping of CE-CD with an abundance of hydrophilic groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups on the surface, provided the CE-CDs with good stability and dispersity when used as an ideal sensing component in aqueous medium.

Optical properties and stability are of great importance to the application of CE-CDs as a fluorescence probe. As shown in Fig. 2a, the absorption peak at 350 nm can be assigned to the n-π * transition of the C＝O on the surface of the CE-CDs, which is consistent with the characteristic absorption regime of the CDs (Duan et al., 2020). The maximum excitation and emission wavelength were 350 and 442 nm, respectively, as measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The stokes shift was as large as 92 nm, which helps to avoid signal overlap and is favorable for sensing applications (Ming et al., 2019). In addition, negligible FI changes were recorded within 60 days of storage at under the dark and 4 °C environment (Fig. 2b), indicating good stability of the CE-CDs. As shown in Fig. S4, CE-CDs lasted for one month at room temperature and under direct sunlight, and the fluorescence intensity decreased by more than 80 %. Along with the change in the excitation wavelength from 280 to 400 nm, an excitation-wavelength dependent FI was observed (Fig. 2c). However, the emission center of the CE-CDs remained consistent under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2d). It is generally agreed that different sizes and multiple surface emissive trap status may lead to inter-system crossing and adjacent vibrational relaxation of the excited electrons, which triggers fluorescence emission of corresponding energy (Hou et al., 2015). Upon the observation of the fluorescence spectra, it can be inferred that non-radiative transitions between the carbon core and surface ligands are likely to account for the stable emission wavelength of the CE-CDs (Zhang and He, 2015). Subsequently, the QY of the CE-CDs were measured to be 72% using quinine sulfate (in 0.1 M H2SO4) as a standard (Fig. S5 & Table S1), which is much higher than that of previous reports. The high QY could be attributed to the highly crystalline structure and uniform surface state of the CE-CDs (Hu et al., 2015), which is consistent with the TEM and spectroscopic results. Upon summarizing the above results, it can be concluded that both high nitrogen doping and a stable surface state account for remarkably high fluorescence monochromaticity, stability, and quantum yield of the as-prepared CE-CDs.

To investigate the response specificity of the CE-CDs to Cr(VI), the fluorescence spectra of the CE-CDs conjugated with different ions, including Cr(VI), were measured. As shown in Fig. 3a, the FI of the CE-CDs incubated with Cr(VI) and Hg(II) decreased significantly than in the other samples. In fact, the interference of Hg(II) to Cr(VI) detection is usually unavoidable due to the closeness of sources. In such cases, the use of a specific masking agent for Hg(II) becomes particularly important to the selectivity of the CDs-based sensing method. Masking agents, such as SDT, LML, or DMPS can be introduced to mask Hg(II) (Fig. S6). The strong blue fluorescence of the CE-CDs was almost completely quenched by the addition of Hg(II). Whereas, the fluorescence of the CE-CDs recovered by 86.0 % in the sample with the added DMPS, while fluorescence recovery in samples to which SDT and LML were added was 13.5 % and 17.6 %, respectively. Along with the addition of DMPS, the CE-CDs showed a specific response to Cr(VI) (Fig. 3b). Therefore, DMPS was identified as an ideal masking agent for Hg(II).

To investigate the optimal masking effect of DMPS, fluorescence emission at 442 nm from the CE-CDs, CE-CDs+Hg(II), and CE-CDs+Cr(VI) were monitored under different DMPS concentrations. As shown in Fig. 3c, the FI of CE-CDs+Hg(II) increased along with the increase in DMPS concentration within a 0.1 M range. Then, FI reached a maximum and tended to be stable, which was almost consistent with the intensity of the CE-CDs solution. The FI of both CE-CDs and CE-CDs+Cr(VI) was steady during this period. Thus, DMPS at a concentration of 0.1 M was selected for use in subsequent experiments. Along with DMPS masking, the CE-CDs responded specifically to Cr(VI) in the presence of Hg(II) or other interference ions (Fig. 3d).

To optimize detection condition, the influence of CE-CDs concentration, pH value, salt concentration, and detection time to the sensing system were investigated. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (FQE), expressed as (F0-F)/F0, was recorded when 50 µM Cr(VI) was successively added to the CE-CDs solution at different concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4a, the FQE reached its maximum value when the CE-CDs concentration was 18 mg/L, indicating a relatively high sensitivity under this concentration. Therefore, the 18 mg/L CE-CDs solution was used in subsequent experiments in this study. Although salt concentration has no impact on the fluorescence stability of the CE-CDs, pH value exerts a remarkable influence on FI (Fig. S7). When the solution is too acidic (pH lower than 4) or too alkaline (pH higher than 11), the FI of the CE-CDs decreases significantly, which may be due to protonation under strong acidic conditions and deprotonation under strong alkali conditions. The reaction kinetics between the CE-CDs and Cr(VI) were studied by monitoring the FQE within 30 min. As shown in Fig. 4b, the FQE increased sharply within 180 s and reached its maximum value, and the FQE kept steady under further incubation. Therefore, 180 s was regarded as the optimal reaction time for sensing.

Subsequently, the quantitative detection of Cr(VI) using the CE-CDs was carried out under the above mentioned experimental conditions. Fig. 4c shows the fluorescence emission spectra of 900 mg/L CE-CDs in the presence of Cr(VI) at different concentrations. The FI of the CE-CDs decreased along with the increase in the Cr(VI) concentration, at which the quenching process can be observed under a UV light (inset of Fig. 4c). When the concentration of Cr(VI) was 2000 μM, the FQE reached a value of 0.95, indicating that the CE-CDs have high detection sensitivity for Cr(VI). In addition, when the concentration of Cr(VI) was 0–500 μM, the FQE showed a good linear relationship with its concentration (R2 =0.9985), while the limit of detection (LOD) was 1.56 μM (S/N = 3) (Fig. 4d). Therefore, for the real samples with a high Cr(VI) limit, CE-CDs can be used at this concentration for visual detection. Whereas, the limiting value of Cr(VI) in some samples, such as drinking water, was rather low. Therefore, reducing the detection range has important practical significance. According to the result shown in Fig. 4e, 18 mg/L CE-CDs could also respond to Cr(VI). A good linear relationship between the FI of the CE-CDs and the Cr(VI) concentration within 5 μM was observed (R2 = 0.9968) (Fig. 4f). The LOD is 23 nM, which is much lower than the limit of Cr(VI) content in drinking water set by the WHO (∼1 µM) (Markiewicz et al., 2016).

The fluorescence quenching mechanism was investigated using UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometers. Fig. 5a displays the UV–vis spectrum of Cr(VI) and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the CE-CDs. It was found that the characteristic absorption band at 350 nm of Cr(VI) overlapped precisely with the excitation wavelength of the CE-CDs. Therefore, the excitation light used for the CE-CDs can be absorbed by Cr(VI) when the CE-CDs are mixed with Cr(VI), resulting in obvious fluorescence quenching of the CE-CDs (Hu et al., 2020). This phenomenon is known as the inner filter effect (IFE), which is likely to occur when the absorption wavelength of the absorber overlaps with the excitation or emission wavelength of the fluorophore (Ming et al., 2019). The IFE effect has been widely used to develop new sensors or to explain the mechanism used in fluorescence-based sensors (Dong et al., 2012; Gu et al., 2017; Li et al., 2018b).

Fig. 5b shows the absorption spectra of the CE-CDs, Cr(VI), and CE-CDs+Cr(VI). Compared with the CE-CDs and Cr(VI), the absorption peak of the CE-CDs+Cr(VI) significantly red-shifted from 350 nm to 362 nm, indicating strong interactions between Cr(VI) and CE-CDs (Liu et al., 2020). The electron transfer between Cr(VI) ions and CE-CDs may be another fluorescence quenching mechanism due to the abundance of functional groups, such as -COOH, -OH, and -NH2, in the CE-CDs. To further understand the interaction between Cr(VI) and the CE-CDs, the fluorescence decay curves of CE-CDs and CE-CDs+Cr(VI) were recorded and the results are displayed in Fig. 5c. Notably, the fluorescence lifetime of CE-CDs+Cr(VI) is 13.41 ns, which is shorter than that of pure CE-CDs (15.10 ns). This phenomenon suggests the occurrence of a dynamic quenching process between Cr(VI) ions and the CE-CDs (Liu et al., 2019). The interaction between CE-CDs and Cr(VI) may lead to a non-radiative recombination of the carbon core excitation, resulting in the reduction of the fluorescence lifetime (Liu et al., 2020). Given the above, the fluorescence quenching mechanism may be concluded as the combination of the IFE effect and dynamic quenching.

Subsequently, the masking mechanism of DMPS to Hg(II) was investigated to provide a general understanding of the masking process. From a molecular structure perspective, DMPS has two sulfydryl groups, which are soft acid groups, while Hg(II) is a soft base. According to the hard-soft-acid-base theory, DMPS may possess a strong affinity for Hg(II), and this is consistent with previous reports (Cardiano et al., 2011). A UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to measure the absorption characteristics of the samples with or without Hg(II) and/or DMPS (Fig. 5d). Compared with pure CE-CDs, the absorption peak of CE-CDs+Hg(II) red-shifted from 350 nm to 360 nm, which is consistent with the phenomenon mentioned in previous reports about the CD response to Hg(II) (Zhai et al., 2014). Upon the addition of DMPS, the absorption band of CE-CDs+Hg(II) shifted back to 350 nm. This result demonstrates that the interaction between Hg(II) ions and CE-CDs is obstructed by DMPS. To further explore the relationship between Hg(II) and DMPS, a head-to-head comparison of the XPS spectra of DMPS and DMPS+Hg(II) was conducted. As shown in Fig. 5d, the S 2p peaks of DMPS at 163.0, 164.0, and 168.0 eV shifted slightly to higher binding energy after the addition of Hg(II). At the same time, the content of each peak belonging to S p3/2, S p1/2, and SOx changed from 34.27 %, 24.43 %, and 41.29to 35.73 %, 24.48 %, and 39.78 %, respectively (Fig. 5f). There was a significantly strong interaction between Hg(II) and DMPS. Upon summarizing the above findings, we hypothesized that a tetra-coordinate complex containing two five-membered rings was formed between DMPS and Hg(II), in which four sulfhydryl groups from two DMPS molecules formed a complex with one Hg(II) ion (Fig. S8).

The application of the CE-CDs-based sensing method for detection of Cr(VI) was evaluated using tap water, river water, and leather, and the results were compared with that measured using standard ion chromatography (IC) ( Table 1). For the detection of Cr(VI) in tap water, the recovery efficiencies and RSDs determined using the CE-CDs ranged from 96.02 % to 102.46 % and 2.57 to 7.88 %, respectively, and are comparable with values determined using the IC method. The river water samples were boiled and filtrated through a 0.45 µm membrane to eliminate impurities. The resultant recovery and RSD of Cr(VI) determined using the CE-CDs also agreed well with those measured using the IC method. As an important object of Cr content monitoring, it is of great practical significance to detect the Cr(VI) concentration in leather samples. In this study, Cr(VI) standard samples were added to the pretreated leather samples for the spiked recovery experiments. Notably, the recovery and RSD Cr(VI) of the leather samples determined using the CE-CDs still remained at ∼100 % and were within 10 %, respectively, indicating a negligible matrix induced interference and good reproducibility of the CE-CDs-based sensing method.

As a newly developed method for Cr(VI) detection, the systematic comparison was conducted between the as-constructed sensing system with existing CDs-based sensors is carried out, and the results are displayed in Table S2. Overall, the Cr(VI) detector prepared in this study combines the advantages of wide linear range and low detection limit on the premise of easy synthesis. More notably, we fully explore the efficiency of our sensing system in the presence of Hg(II) and complex matrix (colored leather sample), and confirm that it possesses remarkable anti-interference capability.

In summary, we synthesized nitrogen-doped CDs (CE-CDs) derived from citric acid and ethylenediamine using a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Taking advantage of their high quantum yield (72 %) and good stability, a CE-CDs-based sensing method for Cr(VI) detection was constructed. In addition, a specific masking agent DMPS for Hg(II) was included in this sensing method considering the interference of Hg(II) in the detection of Cr(VI) in real samples, which is usually unavoidable. The as-constructed sensing method showed high selectivity and accurate determination capability towards Cr(VI) with a large linear concentration range of up to 500 µM and a low detection limit of 23 nM. During the sensing process, the inner filter effect and ion-molecular interaction between Cr(VI) and CE-CDs accounted for the fluorescence quenching mechanism, while efficient masking was attributed to the strong coordination interaction between Hg(II) and DMPS. Ultimately, the CE-CDs-based sensing method was able to perform the quantitative analysis of trace Cr(VI) in tap, river water, and colored leather samples. Overall, this study provides novel perspectives on the development of sensors of high efficiency to overcome interference challenges and is expected to open a new path for personalized, commercialized fluorescence sensor development.

Shengliang Zhao: Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing-Original Draft. Xuandi Lai: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing-Original Draft, Writing-Review & Editing. Feiyan Liu: Methodology, Formal analysis. Liqiong Chen: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Key Field (Rural Revitalization) Project of Colleges and Universities in Guangdong Province, China (2020ZDZX1009), Basic Research Project of Shenzhen Natural Science Foundation (JCYJ20190813103601660), and Shenzhen Technology University Self-Made Experimental Equipment Project (2020XZY009).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ecoenv.2022.114069.
